exemplar.title: To what extent does a luxury fashion brandâs brand identity influence a consumerâs purchase decision?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does a luxury fashion brandâs brand identity influence a consumerâs purchase decision?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and coherent topic communication with a focused investigation purpose
Explicitly stated research question that drives the structure of the essay
Introduction effectively frames the significance of psychological mechanisms in luxury branding
weaknesses: Methodology lacks systematic search strategy and inclusion/exclusion criteria
Limited linkage between certain methodological sections and the research question
Scope remains somewhat diffuse with broad cultural framing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student demonstrates strong focusâevidenced by a clearly stated topic and tightly connected research questionâthe methodology is underdeveloped, lacking rigour in source selection and systematic procedure. Two strands at the 5-band and one at the 3-band average out to a mid-band performance, justifying a 4/6.

strengths: Good subject knowledge with accurate definitions and application of core concepts (e.g., priming, halo effect)
Consistent and mostly accurate use of psychology-specific terminology
Regular citation of peer-reviewed sources to support theoretical explanations
weaknesses: Occasional imprecision (e.g., lowercase author names, conflation of âprimingâ definitions)
Some explanations remain surface-level and drift into marketing commentary
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows solid domain knowledge and correct terminology use, aligning with the 3â4 band across both strands. Minor lapses prevent an âexcellentâ rating, so a 4/6 appropriately reflects mostly coherent understanding and source application.

strengths: Use of a wide range of relevant academic sources (e.g., Bandura, Cialdini, Kahneman)
Clear analytical linkage between theory and concrete luxury brand examples
Acknowledgement of counter-arguments (minimalism, sustainability) enhances evaluation
weaknesses: Limited depth of analysisâconclusions often asserted rather than empirically substantiated
Lack of systematic appraisal of source bias or reliability
Evaluation remains general rather than highly critical or evidence-weighing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three sub-strands fall within the 4â6 band (good research relevance, clear but moderate analysis, reasoned argument with partial evaluation). However, missing rigour and deeper critical engagement place the work at the bottom of this band, warranting a 4/12.

strengths: Conventional EE structure with clear section headings and contents
Appropriate use of in-text citations and a complete reference list
weaknesses: Inconsistent heading hierarchy and section numbering
Table of contents missing page numbers
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the overall layout and conventions are adhered to, formal compliance issuesâparticularly the word-limit breach and inconsistent numberingâundermine presentation quality. Both strands sit around band 1â2, leading to a 2/4 score.

strandSelections.reasoning: Psychology-specific terms such as âauthority bias,â âloss aversion,â and âsocial proofâ are used accurately and repeatedly, and core citations are given.  Minor lapses (e.g., lower-case âmilgramâ; conflation of âprimingâ with general exposure) show some imprecision, but overall terminology is mostly correct and consistent, aligning with the 3-4 band.

Layout featuresâtitle page, table of contents, in-text citations, full reference listâare present and generally correct.  While page numbers are not confirmed in the extract, the presentation appears consistent with academic conventions.  The principal formatting issue is breaching the word limit, but this affects formal compliance more than overall layout appearance.  Given otherwise sound formatting, a score of 3 within the 3-4 band is appropriate.

The student demonstrates good knowledge of key psychological theories (priming, SLT, SIT, halo effect, authority bias) and regularly cites peer-reviewed work.  Concepts are generally explained correctly and applied to luxury examples (e.g., HermÃ¨s Birkin, Balenciaga Triple S).  Some explanations remain surface-level and occasionally slide into marketing commentary without deep psychological elaboration, preventing an âexcellent / consistently coherentâ judgement.

The methodology is articulated (secondary literature review, psychological framework, scope/limitations, ethical considerations) and sources appear mostly scholarly.  However, no systematic search strategy, inclusion criteria, or critical source appraisal is presented, and there is no primary research to strengthen validity.  Thus the approach is âmostly completeâ with some informed source selection but lacks the rigour expected for the top band.

The student clearly states the topic in the title and repeats it in the introduction, explaining why the psychology of luxury branding is worth studying and outlining the main psychological constructs to be examined.  The introduction effectively frames the investigation as an exploration of âhow luxury brands shape consumer behaviour,â signalling a purposeful and coherent focus.  Minor issuesâsuch as the slightly over-long word count and a broad framing of âluxury fashionâ across culturesâcreate some diffuseness, but overall the topic is conveyed accurately and with a strong sense of direction, matching the 5-6 descriptor.

A wide range of academic and industry sources underpin the discussion, and almost every claim is referenced.  The sources chosen (e.g., peer-reviewed journals, seminal theorists such as Bandura, Cialdini, Kahneman) are relevant to the research question; nevertheless, some citations are dated marketing texts and the cultural/geographical limitation noted by the student is not fully mitigated.  These qualities fit comfortably within the âgood / mostly relevantâ 4-6 band, meriting a mid-band score of 5.

The research questionââTo what extent does a luxury fashion brandâs brand identity influence a consumerâs purchase decision?ââis explicitly stated, referred to throughout, and consistently drives the subsections (priming, social proof, halo effect, SIT).  Although the phrase âto what extentâ is quite broad, the essay narrows the scope to psychological mechanisms within luxury fashion, demonstrating an effective connection between question and discussion.  Occasional drift into marketing strategy (e.g., tiered exclusivity) shows partial over-breadth, so a solid 5 rather than 6 is warranted.

The essay follows a conventional EE structure with title page, contents, numbered sections, analysis, conclusion, and reference list; headings facilitate navigation.  Nevertheless, the work exceeds the 4 000-word limit and some section numbering is inconsistent, indicating minor structural weaknesses.  It therefore âclearly adopts an appropriate structureâ overall but with small shortcomings, warranting the lower half of the top band.

The student offers a logical progressionâadvantages of biases, limitations, future challengesâand acknowledges counter-arguments such as minimalism and sustainability.  Evaluation is present but tends to be general (âbrands must adapt or risk losing relevanceâ) with limited critical depth or weighing of evidence.  Argument structure is sound yet not fully critical or well-substantiated, aligning with a 4 in the 4-6 band.

The essay links theories to concrete brand examples and occasionally contrasts traditional and emerging consumer values, showing clear, mostly supported analysis.  However, much of the writing remains explanatory; data from cited studies are rarely interrogated, and conclusions (e.g., brands risk devaluation) are asserted rather than evidenced with empirical findings.  Depth is therefore moderate rather than comprehensive, placing the work at the lower end of the 4-6 range.

annotations.comment: The title and focus clearly convey the subject and purpose of the investigation, establishing a coherent direction for the essay.
The research question is explicitly stated and directly linked to the subsequent analysis, ensuring clarity of focus throughout the essay.
The word count exceeds the 4,000âword limit, which affects compliance with EE regulations; consider condensing sections to meet the maximum word requirement.
The table of contents is organized with clear section headings, facilitating easy navigation and reflecting a conventional EE structure.
Consider adding page numbers next to each entry in the table of contents to improve reader orientation and align with academic conventions.
The introduction succinctly frames the research purpose and highlights the significance of exploring psychological mechanisms in luxury branding.
While the introduction outlines key theories, it could benefit from defining the specific cultural or demographic scope to strengthen the precision of the context.
The methodology lacks a description of systematic search procedures or inclusion/exclusion criteria, limiting transparency regarding source selection.
Consider including a critical appraisal process for sources, assessing reliability and bias to enhance the rigour of your secondary research methodology.
Acknowledging the Western focus and absence of primary data demonstrates critical reflection on limitations, enhancing the credibility of the analysis.
Whilst ethical concerns are noted, explicitly detailing criteria for evaluating source credibility would strengthen the argument for unbiased data usage.
The student demonstrates solid domain knowledge by accurately defining core concepts such as brand identity and the consumer decisionâmaking process with appropriate references.
Link this section more explicitly back to the research question to reinforce cohesion and show how brand identity directly influences consumer decisions.
The explanation of priming is thorough and effectively supported by examples of luxury brand visual cues, showing clear understanding of cognitive processes.
The Social Learning Theory example lacks a specific citation for the Chanel ambassador case, reducing the traceability of the evidence; include precise sourcing.
The discussion of social proof and influencer endorsement is wellâapplied to luxury contexts, showing good analytical linkage to the research question.
This section could emphasize how scarcity strategies directly answer the extent to which brand identity shapes purchase decisions to maintain focus.
The analysis of emerging trends in minimalism and sustainability demonstrates critical evaluation of counterâperspectives, enriching the discussion depth.
Consider integrating empirical data or study findings to substantiate claims about generational shifts, strengthening the evidence base under analysis.
The description of the Halo Effect is accurate and supported by concrete examples, illustrating effective application of psychological concepts.
The lowercase presentation of âmilgramâ is inconsistent with academic conventions; ensure proper capitalization of author names and terms.
The account of Authority Bias is insightful and effectively linked to brand heritage examples, demonstrating solid understanding of persuasive mechanisms.
Reduce repetitive phrasing of âbrand identityâ within this passage; varying terminology will improve readability and stylistic precision.
Section numbering shifts from <h4> to <h3> here, creating inconsistency in heading hierarchy; align header levels for clear structure.
This paragraph is dense and could be broken into smaller subâparagraphs to enhance readability and meet academic layout conventions.
